sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 30-year-old female, presents with a chronic history of asthma symptoms, specifically coughing and wheezing, which she describes as always present, with exacerbations occurring every month or every other month for the past six months.
She has required frequent visits to urgent care and her primary care physician, often receiving antibiotics and steroids, including a high-dose prednisone taper pack.
She has also had one hospitalization due to asthma symptoms at the age of eight.
The patient uses a blue inhaler, identified as a Ventolin (albuterol) inhaler, which she finds effective, but uses it almost daily, often two puffs at a time, and also occasionally at night.
She reports that her symptoms improve with use of this inhaler.
She mentions that her symptoms worsen when she gets sick with a cold and also when she visits her aunt's house due to her cat.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of asthma, with symptoms dating back six months, and one hospitalization at the age of eight.
Review of systems: The patient reports no other symptoms or issues, including no seasonal allergies.
Current medications: Ventolin (albuterol) inhaler, two puffs every 4-6 hours as needed; Flovent (fluticasone) 110 mcg, one puff twice daily.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/82, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on room air.
Physical exam: The patient's lung exam reveals diminished lung sounds with occasional slight expiratory wheeze bilaterally.
The heart exam is regular rate and rhythm with no ectopic beats, murmurs, or gallops.
The neck exam is supple with midline trachea and no lymphadenopathy.
The carotid bruit is absent.
The hands have strong bilateral pulses with no clubbing and brisk capillary refill.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has asthma, moderate intermittent, confirmed.
Asthma; Status: confirmed.
Plan:
I started the patient on Flovent (fluticasone) 110 mcg, one puff twice daily, to be used consistently to prevent daily symptoms and reduce the need for her albuterol inhaler.
I instructed her to continue using her Ventolin (albuterol) inhaler as needed, one to two puffs, and to wait and see how her breathing is before taking additional puffs.
I will put together an asthma action plan to help manage her symptoms and address specific triggers, such as her cat.
I will see her in 30 days to review her progress and adjust her treatment plan as needed.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in 30 days to review her progress and adjust her treatment plan as needed.
